retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: A tendon advancement of over 1 cm carries the risk of quadriga. The term quadriga refers to an inability to flex the digits adjacent to the involved digit from increased tension over the repaired tendon. [18] Other surgical complications include infection, skin necrosis, tendon repair rupture, nail matrix injury, and adhesions. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be aware of chronic pain and the loss of grip strength as consequences of delayed management of jersey finger injuries. Although the nonunion rate in bony-avulsion type jersey finger injuries is low, smoking cessation is a recommendation for fracture healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Jersey finger can happen in any digit and represents the most common closed flexor tendon injury. [7] The ring finger is the most common involved digit (75% of the cases). When performing gripping, the ring fingertip becomes the most prominent digit in the vast majority of the population. Also, the ring finger is bound on both sides by lumbrical muscles, making it more prone to hyperextension injuries. There are reports that the load to failure of the FDP tendon needed for the ring finger is much less than other digits. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Finger injuries account for 38% of all acute upper extremity injuries. [5] A recent study showed that tendon injuries in the hand occur at a rate of 33.2 per 100000 person-years. Only 4% of these injuries occur in flexor tendon zone I (distal to the insertion of flexor digitorum superficialis on the middle phalanx). [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Jersey finger injuries may get dismissed as minor sprains or phalanx fractures. There are several reports of athletes “playing through” the injury, even at a competitive level. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The distal phalanx suffers exposure to substantial forces during pull-away mechanisms. Flexor digitorium distal avulsion commonly presents in young athletes, especially in contact sports. [4] The mechanism of injury typically results from forceful extension of a flexed digit. A common example is grabbing the jersey of an opposing player to make a tackle during American football or rugby, resulting in a forced extension of the flexor digitorium profundus tendon during maximum contraction of the muscle belly.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: It is crucial to have a high level of suspicion for a jersey finger in athletes with finger pain and to associate these injuries to sport-related trauma. Pain and tenderness of the volar aspect of the injured finger are the common presentations. In a resting position, the injured finger will usually remain in extension compared to the other digits. Sometimes, the retracted tendon can be palpated proximally to the avulsion. Flexion of the DIP joint is absent. Grip and flexion against resistance will usually cause pain.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Staging
retrieved_docs.content: Jersey Finger Injury Classification [9] Type 1: Severe avulsion. The tendon retracts into the palm. Blood supply is severely compromised. Type 2: The tendon retracts but remains at the A3 pulley (proximal interphalangeal joint). Type 3: The avulsion includes a bony fragment. Both tendon and fracture fragment remain at the A4 pulley. Type 4: Rare injury, defined by the presence of both a fracture and a tendon avulsion from the bony fragment. The tendon can retract into the palm.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the pathophysiology of distal flexor digitorium tendon avulsion. Identify the indications for acute treatment of jersey finger injuries. Explain the common physical exam findings associated with jersey finger. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team to reach an early diagnosis of a jersey finger and avoid chronic functional limitations with difficult treatment options. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Jersey finger (also known as rugby finger) is an avulsion of the flexor digitorium profundus tendon (FDP) from its distal insertion on the distal phalanx (zone I). [1] [2] [3] The mechanism of injury is typically a forced extension of a flexed digit, such as trying to grab the jersey of an opponent during a high-speed sporting event. On exam, the affected digit remains in slight extension compared to the other digits. No active flexion of the distal interphalangeal joint (DIP) is possible. Treatment is surgical with the treatment plan dictated by the acuity of injury, the zone of injury, and any associated fracture.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although physical examination should be enough to reach the diagnosis of jersey finger, X-rays and ultrasound may play an important role. Plain radiographs are mandatory to rule out fractures. Antero-posterior and lateral views may reveal a bony fragment, if present. Ultrasound may be useful to asses tendon anatomy in cases without fracture. In chronic injuries, ultrasound becomes crucial to evaluate tendon retraction and guide further treatment. MRI is rarely performed but can be used to determine the increased tendon-bone distance more accurately. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic: over 3 months after injury. Two-stage tendon grafting (if the full range of motion is present). DIP joint arthrodesis (if chronic stiffness is present). Joint arthrodesis requires careful discussion with each patient. The distal interphalangeal joint motion may be essential for some patients (occupation and hobbies), and tendon reconstruction may become a valid alternative. Unfortunately, tendon reconstruction requires a significant time commitment from the patient to achieve a successful long-term rehabilitation outcome.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Early diagnosis leads to expedited treatment with excellent functional outcomes. Surgery within 10 days after the injury correlates with excellent patient-reported outcomes. [15] Patients may return to sports with both full active functional range of motion and the absence of pain (approximately 8 to 12 weeks). Functional consequences of impaired DIP joint motion includes loss of dexterity and loss of pinch strength. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Finger Dislocation -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint dislocations are common in athletic settings and are sometimes referred to as “coach’s finger” due to their frequent occurrence and tendency to be underestimated or improperly managed in sports environments. (see Image. Proximal Interphalangeal [PIP] Joint Dorsal Dislocation, Radiograph). Similar to MCP dislocations, PIP dislocations are typically dorsal. Dorsal dislocation results from longitudinal compression and hyperextension, commonly by a ball hitting the fingertip. These most commonly occur at the middle finger and are associated with volar plate, collateral ligament, and dorsal joint capsular injuries. Swan neck deformities may occur in dorsal dislocations from volar plate injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Finger Dislocation -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, dislocations of the fingers are fairly common injuries, with an estimated incidence of 11.1 per 100,000 person-years in the United States. [10] Most of these injuries involve the PIP and DIP joints, while MCP dislocations are associated with an incidence of less than 1 per 100,000 person-years annually. Most dislocations occur in adolescent or young adult men between the ages of 15 and 19 and commonly occur in basketball and football players.

retrieved_docs.title: Jersey Finger -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative functional and aesthetic results depend on accurate reduction, repair quality, and an adequate rehabilitation protocol. Prevention of scar contracture formation is crucial to maintain finger function. [16] [17]

Wrist injury is classified both as fractured wrist bone and as a closed distal forearm fracture, reinforcing the hierarchical relationship between wrist, hand, and general limb fracture classifications.

These interrelationships underscore how vascular complications, self‑inflicted wounds, and a wide spectrum of musculoskeletal trauma—particularly fractures of the clavicle, upper and lower limbs, and associated injuries from falls or traffic accidents—are interconnected, informing accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 169

Collectively, these relationships define a structured, multilingual taxonomy of accident‑ and injury‑related terms that enables consistent coding, comprehensive clinical documentation, and robust epidemiologic analysis of patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: in patients with diabetes mellitus, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population. Clinically, diabetes mellitus is associated with several serious complications. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically linked to diabetes, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy. Peripheral vascular disease frequently leads to lower‑extremity amputations, and the hierarchical structure of these procedures shows that “amputation of lower extremity” includes specific child procedures such as foot amputation (بتر القدم), below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), and digit amputation (بتر أصبع القدم). Both digit amputation and below‑knee amputation are themselves clinically associated with diabetes mellitus, underscoring the disease’s impact on limb viability. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is associated with the morphology termed النَخَر and is linked to the broader category of lower‑extremity amputation, indicating that stump

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 95
retrieved_docs.content: relationship, is linked to hand fracture (كسر اليد), which resides under both fractures of limbs (كسر في الأطراف) and fractures/dislocations of limbs (كسور وانخلاعات الأطراف). Wrist injury (إصابة في المعصم) is therefore synonymous with both عظم رسغ مكسور and the closed lower‑forearm fracture definition. Head injury trauma frequently co‑occurs with shoulder fracture (كسر الكتف). The generic unspecified injuries category (إصابات، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) also encompasses musculoskeletal injury, wound, and concomitant fracture (مُتَلاَزِمَةٌ هَرْسِيَّة), illustrating the hierarchical and associative structure that supports accurate classification, diagnosis, and coordinated patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: clavicle, femur, humerus, radius, scapula, and ulna—to the systemic disease category of osteoporosis or low bone density. “Fracture of neck of femur (hip)” itself is classified within the same osteoporosis SMQ, underscoring low bone mass as a key risk factor. Internal‑fixation procedures (identified as “تثبيت داخلي للكسر”) are associated with multiple treatment categories: “fracture or dislocation of hip and femur,” “fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur),” and “fracture or dislocation of radius and ulna.” These associations emphasize that bone quality influences surgical decision‑making for both hip and peripheral limb injuries. Within the osteoporosis SMQ, additional members such as “جراحة مفصل الورك” (hip joint surgery), “إعاضة رأس الفخذ” (hip head prosthesis), and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2097
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures and dislocations of the limbs (كسور وانخلاعات الأطراف) represent a broad category of musculoskeletal injuries that includes hand fractures (كسر اليد). Hand fractures are a specific subset of upper‑limb fractures (كسر الطرف العلوي), and within this subset the injury of a broken wrist bone (عظم رسغ مكسور) is a more precise diagnosis. Consequently, a broken wrist bone is both a hand fracture and an upper‑limb fracture, all of which fall under the overarching classification of limb fractures and dislocations. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians accurately diagnose, code, and manage extremity injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: type of hand fracture. Wrist injury (إصابة في المعصم) is both an instance of عظم رسغ مكسور and a closed distal‑forearm fracture, reinforcing the connection between wrist injuries and upper‑limb fractures. Clavicle fracture (كسر الترقوة) is grouped with other limb fractures (كسر في الأطراف), closely related to shoulder fractures and femur fractures, and is commonly observed in the context of high‑energy mechanisms such as motor‑vehicle collisions, often listed alongside Dutch “Fractuur van schouder en bovenarm” and English “fracture of upper limb.” The knowledge network therefore links vascular disease of the extremities to gangrenous outcomes and integrates a wide array of polytraumatic injuries—face, chest, head, wrist, hand, shoulder and lower‑limb injuries—within the etiologic contexts of falls and traffic accidents, highlighting the anatomical, pathological and procedural relationships that are essential for comprehensive assessment, diagnosis and management of patients with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2839
retrieved_docs.content: بتر فوق الركبة (above‑knee amputation) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 1 (type 1 diabetes). Patients with type 1 diabetes have a heightened risk of severe peripheral vascular disease and foot complications, which can progress to the need for an above‑knee amputation. Recognizing this association is essential for clinicians to implement early screening, aggressive glycemic control, and multidisciplinary foot‑care strategies aimed at preventing major limb loss and improving overall patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.content: diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: are also described. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is associated with the broader category of lower‑extremity amputation and exhibits a specific morphological feature termed النَخَر. This relationship emphasizes the risk of tissue necrosis at the stump site, which requires careful postoperative monitoring in diabetic patients. Overall, the interconnected knowledge illustrates how diabetes mellitus serves as a central disease entity linked to a spectrum of surgical, ophthalmic, genetic, pharmacologic, and postoperative considerations, all of which are essential for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

Additionally, “Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction” is identified as a versatile internal fixation technique applicable to fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur, the radius and ulna, and other lower‑extremity sites, underscoring its role across multiple skeletal regions. Collectively, these entities illustrate an integrated framework for fracture fixation, deformity correction, growth modulation, prosthetic revision, and preventive care in thigh and leg surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 261
retrieved_docs.content: and “كسور متعددة” (multiple fractures) illustrate the spectrum of interventions and clinical manifestations that arise in patients with compromised bone health. By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 274
retrieved_docs.content: Upper‑limb fractures (كسر الطرف العلوي) are organized under upper‑limb fracture categories and include distal forearm closed fractures; wrist bone fractures are children of hand fractures, and the inverse_isa relationship confirms this nesting. Injuries not classified elsewhere (إصابات، غير مصنف في موضع آخر) encompass musculoskeletal injury (إصابة العضلات والعظام), wound (جرح), and concomitant fracture (مُتَلاَزِمَةٌ هَرْسِيَّة). Shoulder fractures frequently co‑occur with head injury trauma. Recognizing these cause‑effect, co‑occurrence, and hierarchical relationships supports comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and coordinated management of patients with multiple traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2560
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated blood ketones in diabetic patients (ارتفاع الكيتونات في الدم عند مرضى السكري) are clinically associated with foot amputation (بتر القدم). Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb (Necrosis of amputation stump of right lower limb) is linked to amputation of the lower extremity and typically exhibits gangrene morphology (النَخَر). The broader category “amputation of lower extremity” encompasses specific procedures such as foot amputation (بتر القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة). Together, these connections illustrate how metabolic disturbances in diabetes can precipitate severe lower‑extremity outcomes, how necrotic changes at a stump often present as gangrene, and why early recognition and intervention are essential to prevent progression to major limb loss.
